dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: This dates the witness, not the composition of each saying.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century English parchment manuscript of i + 103 leaves. Its second cataloged item, fols. 13–31 with an inferred endpoint, contains English proverbs with Latin hexameter counterparts, some Latin-only proverbs, and a rough alphabetical order; the beginning is imperfect.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Douce 52

notes: The binding is described as leather, perhaps over wooden boards, later recovered in green leather.
At least three hands occur; catalog Hand A writes articles 1–3 and therefore includes the target item.
Penwork decoration extends across fols. 1r–25v and therefore overlaps part of the target.
Francis Douce owned the manuscript and bequeathed it to the Bodleian in 1834.

rights.basis: The medieval text is public domain; rights in modern photographs, catalog text, and supplied reproductions are distinct layers.
rights.component: Medieval linguistic content represented by the witness

bibliographic.citation: Nicole Bozon, Les contes moralisés de Nicole Bozon, frère mineur, publiés pour la première fois d'après les manuscrits de Londres et de Cheltenham, ed. Lucy Toulmin Smith and Paul Meyer (Paris: Firmin Didot, 1889).
bibliographic.title: Les contes moralisés de Nicole Bozon, frère mineur

entities.description: The first edition of Nicole Bozon's Contes moralisés, based principally on Gray's Inn MS 12, collated against the former Phillipps 8336 (now Add MS 46919), and followed by the partial Latin translation in Harley MS 1288. Förster selected ten English passages from printed pp. 12, 20, 23, 54, 117, 145, 151, 205, and 209.
entities.label: Smith and Meyer, Les contes moralisés de Nicole Bozon (1889)

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1889 and is in the public domain in the United States.

next_steps: Request each complete current physical object, not only target leaves or short extracts, including bindings, spines, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, edges, and detached matter where offered.
Map target passages separately: Douce fols. 13–31, Gray's Inn fols. 15r–49v, Add fols. 120r–153v, and Harley current fols. 91r–105v.
Retain repository inventories, quotations, delivery correspondence, and exact license or permission terms with any supplied files.
openQuestions.question: Can the Bodleian, Gray's Inn, and British Library supply or authorize complete color facsimiles of MS. Douce 52, Gray's Inn MS 12, Add MS 46919, and Harley MS 1288, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern each delivery?

next_steps: Inspect complete color images of fols. 30–32 and adjacent quire evidence.
Confirm whether Förster's 31a maps to modern 31r and whether the catalog's inferred endpoint includes any part of 31v.
Keep the competing locators explicit until image or repository confirmation resolves them.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact ending side and physical boundary of the Douce proverb sequence described by the catalog as fols. 13–31 with an inferred endpoint and by Förster as fols. 13a–31a?

next_steps: Consult current manuscript foliation images or obtain repository confirmation before inferring an offset.
Preserve Smith and Meyer's historical locators and dates as edition-layer claims.
Use current repository locators for access requests while recording all delivery labels verbatim.
openQuestions.question: How do Smith and Meyer's legacy Harley locus fols. 112–125 and date of the late fourteenth century correspond to the current British Library locus fols. 91r–105v and fifteenth-century date, and should any Add MS 46919 locator or date differences also be normalized?

next_steps: Report the mismatch with the stable item identifier, encoded title/byline, and verified printed citation.
Do not model the incorrect header as an alternate source edition.
Recheck the live item after any provider response.
openQuestions.question: Will the University of Michigan correct CME00139's current item-header title and citation so that discovery metadata identifies Förster's 1906 Douce article?

next_steps: Build a numbered Förster-to-DIMEV concordance from incipits, explicits, loci, and the complete target folios.
Retain record-759's two witness occurrences rather than deduplicating the manuscript evidence.
Do not assign one DIMEV record to the full edited selection.
openQuestions.question: Which of Förster's 150 numbered Douce units correspond exactly to the 42 distinct DIMEV v1.0 records, and how should prose commonplaces, Latin-only manuscript material, and repeated verse records be represented in a future item-level concordance?

relations.subjectLabel: DIMEV v1.0 records attested in MS. Douce 52

reviewNotes: The cataloged subject is Förster's two-stream edited selection, not a single stable abstract medieval work and not the complete contents of MS. Douce 52.
The 150 main numbered units include 37 units in which Förster retained Latin; the ten addenda are a second independently numbered section sourced through Smith and Meyer's edition.

Förster's mid-fifteenth-century scribal date, London-language description, and hypothetical older north-west Midland or southern northern exemplar are editorial hypotheses. They are not silently converted into a work composition date or place.

The current Bodleian record gives the target as fols. 13–31 with an inferred endpoint, whereas Förster says fols. 13a–31a. A complete target-leaf facsimile is needed to resolve the exact side and physical endpoint.

Smith and Meyer's legacy Harley locus, fols. 112–125, conflicts with the current British Library locus, fols. 91r–105v. The two locators are preserved without inferring an unverified foliation offset.

The University of Michigan item header currently misidentifies CME00139 as Frühmittelenglische Sprichwörter in Englische Studien 31 (1900), while the displayed content and repository XML are Förster's 1906 Douce article. This is a discovery-layer mismatch, not a different source lineage.
DIMEV v1.0 links MS. Douce 52 to 42 distinct verse records in 43 witness occurrences; these item-level records do not make one DIMEV identifier for Förster's whole 150-unit selection.
Whole-object facsimile completeness is evaluated against each current physical manuscript, not against the few target leaves or passages selected by an edition.

lineage.summary: CME00139 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of selected portions of Max Förster's 1906 article Die mittelenglische Sprichwörtersammlung in Douce 52. The article's main section edits 150 numbered Middle English proverbs and commonplaces from Bodleian Library MS. Douce 52, fols. 13–31, while retaining Latin counterparts in only 37 numbered units. Its addenda excerpt ten English sayings or fable passages from Lucy Toulmin Smith and Paul Meyer's 1889 edition of Nicole Bozon's Contes moralisés, whose base and comparison witnesses are Gray's Inn MS 12, British Library Add MS 46919, and Harley MS 1288. The XML encodes the article title and byline plus printed pp. 44–60, but omits Förster's introduction on pp. 40–44 and relocates the title and byline from p. 40 to the p. 44 position. Complete scans and OCR of both printed source volumes, the official Bodleian TEI description, and DIMEV v1.0 are cached with fixity. No complete public facsimile was found for any of the four manuscripts.

catalogingSubject.label: Die mittelenglische Sprichwörtersammlung in Douce 52
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Max Förster's 1906 editorial selection as encoded in CME00139, not one stable medieval authored work. Its first section numbers 150 Middle English proverb and commonplace units from Bodleian Library MS. Douce 52 and retains Latin hexameter counterparts in only 37 of those units. Its addenda select ten English proverbs, phrases, and fable-like passages from Lucy Toulmin Smith and Paul Meyer's 1889 edition of Nicole Bozon's Contes moralisés. The record keeps those two source streams, Förster's modern German framing and notes, the physical manuscripts, the two printed editions, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML as distinct layers.

contentParts.label: Douce 52 proverb and commonplace selection

locators.value: fols. 13r–31 (catalog endpoint inferred; Förster uses fols. 13a–31a)

notes: Fourteen letter divisions survive; the collection begins with the last C proverb and is imperfect at the beginning.
The sequence is roughly alphabetized by the initial sound of the key word in the Latin versions, not by a modern English keyword order.
Förster's numbering is an editorial access structure and is not treated as 150 separately authored works.
contentParts.summary: One hundred fifty numbered Middle English proverb and commonplace units edited from the bilingual sequence in MS. Douce 52.

contentParts.label: Retained Latin hexameter counterparts

locators.value: Latin hexameter pairing layer within fols. 13r–31

notes: The count is nested within, not additional to, the 150 numbered main units.

The manuscript catalog describes English proverbs with Latin hexameter translations and some Latin-only proverbs; the present part records only the Latin actually retained in Förster's encoded selection.
contentParts.summary: Latin counterparts retained by Förster in 37 of the 150 numbered Douce units: all first ten and later examples he judged useful for understanding.

contentParts.label: Bozon addenda
notes: Items 1–8 occur in the Smith–Meyer Anglo-French edition text; items 9–10 are taken from its printed Harley Latin-translation section, which preserves the English phrases.
Förster's Chelt. and Harl. variants reproduce Smith and Meyer's apparatus. No direct consultation of Gray's Inn MS 12, Add MS 46919, or Harley MS 1288 by Förster is asserted.
The ten readings map respectively to Smith–Meyer printed pp. 12, 20, 23, 23, 54, 117, 145, 151, 205, and 209.
contentParts.summary: Ten English proverbs, phrases, and fable-like passages excerpted by Förster from Smith and Meyer's edition of the Contes moralisés tradition.

contentParts.label: Förster's editorial framing and apparatus

locators.value: BODY/DIV1/HEAD, BYLINE, DIV1[2]/P[1–2], and NOTE elements
title and byline on p. 40; encoded notes and addenda framing on pp. 44–60

notes: The XML omits Förster's introduction on printed pp. 40–44 up to the start of proverb 1, while relocating the title and byline from p. 40 to its opening PB N=44 position.
German editorial prose and short quoted Old French passages are not cataloged as the languages or form of the medieval selected sayings.
contentParts.summary: The article title and byline, two German addenda-introduction paragraphs, and 139 encoded foot or marginal notes containing source citations, variants, corrections, and comments.

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving Douce witness. Förster's more specific mid-fifteenth-century scribal date and hypothetical older exemplar are not converted into composition dates.

Gray's Inn MS 12 is mid-fourteenth-century; Add MS 46919 is currently dated before 1333; Harley MS 1288 is currently dated to the fifteenth century. The broad span avoids silently applying one witness date to all ten English excerpts.

dateStatements.display: Fourteenth- and fifteenth-century witness attestations; individual passages vary

notes: This is the date of Förster's two-stream editorial selection, not the composition date of the medieval sayings.

extent.description: Two independently numbered sections: 150 Douce units and ten Bozon-derived addenda units. The 250 XML LB milestones (233 in the first section, 17 in the addenda) include Middle English, retained Latin, and typographic display rows; they are not asserted as 250 verse lines. Förster retains Latin counterparts within 37 of the 150 main units.

formStatements.description: The selection mixes couplet-like and other verse sayings, single-sentence commonplaces, Latin hexameters, short embedded phrases, and modern prose framing. XML LB milestones are display rows and cannot safely be equated with verse lines.

assertion.notes: Förster calls only items 1, 3, 4, and 9 genuine proverbs, perhaps also item 7, and suggests that other passages may derive from lost English fable versions.

genres.label: Embedded sayings and possible fable residues

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Förster retained Latin counterparts in 37 of the 150 numbered Douce units and stated that he normally omitted them unless they aided understanding.

languageStatements.label: Old French
notes: The ten selected addenda texts themselves are Middle English; the surrounding Contes moralisés is not silently counted as encoded subject content.

scope.description: Short source-language quotations inside Förster's addenda commentary and notes

notes: The University of Michigan item's current catalog header incorrectly labels CME00139 as Frühmittelenglische Sprichwörter and gives an unknown publication statement, while the item contents display Förster's correct title and text; that interface mismatch is not propagated into the descriptive title or source lineage.
The XML's TEMPHEAD records a 2016 review that moved or added structure and removed an initial prose fragment; it is an encoding-maintenance statement, not a medieval or 1906 editorial event.

Förster's printed article begins on p. 40, but CME00139 encodes only the relocated title and byline plus the text from mid-p. 44 through p. 60; the unencoded introduction on pp. 40–44 remains part of the source edition, not the corpus work boundary.
Manuscript dates, physical origins, scribal hands, and dialect observations are scoped to witnesses; publication dates and German prose are scoped to editorial and digital layers.

next_steps: Retain Förster's 150-unit edited selection as the current cataloging subject.
Compare each unit against DIMEV record scope and manuscript loci before creating independent related-work records.
Do not assign one DIMEV ID to the entire selection.
openQuestions.question: Which of the 150 Douce units should eventually receive independent work-level identities, and how do those units relate to the forty-two DIMEV records and one duplicated Douce occurrence?

next_steps: Request a complete color reproduction of the current MS. Douce 52 object and map the work separately to the target leaves.
Preserve the catalog's inferred fol. 31 endpoint and Förster's fol. 31a statement until images or repository clarification resolve the side.
Do not convert scribal-language observations into a composition-place facet.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete facsimile confirm the target's exact final folio side, physical boundaries, hand continuity, and the relationship between Förster's London/North-West-Midlands linguistic observations and the current catalog's broader description?

next_steps: Preserve former Phillipps 8336 alongside current Add MS 46919.
Preserve Smith and Meyer's Harley fols. 112–125 and late-fourteenth-century description separately from the current fols. 91r–105v and fifteenth-century catalog date.
Use complete manuscript images or repository foliation histories before inferring any numerical offset.
openQuestions.question: How should the legacy Smith–Meyer locators and dates for the Cheltenham and Harley witnesses be concorded with current British Library foliation and dating?

assertion.notes: Witness production, scribal dialect, and hypothetical exemplar origin remain separate from work composition.

scope.description: The medieval English sayings in both editorial source streams

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English proverb makers and transmitters
notes: Nicole Bozon is associated with the surrounding Contes moralisés, but Förster himself allows that several English addenda may preserve material from lost English fable versions; the English snippets are therefore not assigned wholesale to Bozon.

scope.description: Anonymous medieval wording represented in the 150 Douce units and ten Bozon-derived addenda items
The Douce 52 section; Varnhagen independently checked proofs against the manuscript
German introduction, two addenda framing paragraphs, and comparative or textual notes
Proof-sheet checking against MS. Douce 52 and provision of Latin readings
Emendations and comments explicitly credited to Bradley in Förster's notes

subjects.label: Speech, truth, reputation, and civic responsibility
Household, labor, food, and material life

summaries.value: A two-stream editorial gathering of concise Middle English counsel. The Douce section ranges across generosity, friendship, speech, work, hunger, household management, law, trust, reputation, and the reversals of fortune; selected Latin hexameters provide a bilingual pairing layer. The addenda extract ten English sayings and fable-like phrases embedded in the Anglo-French Contes moralisés tradition.

The first DIV1 contains 150 numbered AB units organized under fourteen surviving letter headings (D, E, F, G, I, L, M, N, O, P, R, S, T, V), with item 1 preceding the D division because the manuscript is imperfect at the beginning. The second DIV1 contains two German framing paragraphs and ten separately numbered addenda units. The XML contains 160 LABEL and AB elements, 250 LB milestones, 17 printed PB milestones, and 139 NOTE elements.

The edition-level boundary is necessary because the 150-unit Douce sequence is a witness-specific bilingual collection, DIMEV v1.0 distributes its verse material across forty-two distinct records and forty-three Douce witness occurrences, and the ten addenda are separately selected through Smith and Meyer's printed edition. No single DIMEV identifier, manuscript date, dialect, place, or authorship is assigned to the whole CME selection.

notes: This is Förster's printed article title. The repository XML relocates it from printed p. 40 to the position immediately after its PB N=44 milestone.

assertion.notes: A close English translation supplied for discovery; it does not imply that the addenda derive from Douce 52.

titles.value: The Middle English Proverb Collection in Douce 52
He þat doth as can, Blame hym no man
First loke and aftirward lepe; Avyse the welle, or thow speke

notes: The source prints no autonomous formal title for this section; the label summarizes Förster's two opening German paragraphs.
